5e. Treemap Visualisation with R

Author

Lei Yuhe

Published

May 14, 2026

Modified

May 17, 2026

Overview

A treemap is a space-filling visualisation that encodes hierarchical data as nested rectangles. Two visual channels are used simultaneously:

  • Rectangle size: quantitative variable (e.g., total units sold)
  • Rectangle colour: a second quantitative variable (e.g., median unit price)

Treemaps are especially effective for displaying part-to-whole relationships across multiple levels of a hierarchy, such as property transactions grouped by region planning area, and then project name.

This chapter covers three approaches:

Approach Package Function(s)
Static treemap (full-featured) treemap treemap()
Static treemap (ggplot2-based) treemapify geom_treemap() + ggplot2
Interactive treemap d3treeR d3tree()

Installing and Launching R Packages

pacman::p_load(treemap, treemapify, tidyverse)

d3treeR is not on CRAN and must be installed from GitHub:

# install.packages("devtools")          # skip if already installed
library(devtools)
install_github("timelyportfolio/d3treeR")
library(d3treeR)
Package Role
treemap Full-featured static treemaps with 43+ arguments
treemapify Treemaps as ggplot2 layers; supports ggplot2 ecosystem
d3treeR Interactive, zoomable treemaps built on D3.js
tidyverse Data import (readr) and wrangling (dplyr)

Data Wrangling

The dataset is realis2018.csv, private property transaction records from Singapore’s Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA) REALIS portal for 2018.

Importing the data set

realis2018 <- read_csv("data/realis2018.csv")

Data Wrangling and Manipulation

The raw data is at transaction level (one row per transaction), which is too disaggregated for a treemap. We need to aggregate it to project level, computing:

  • Total Unit Sold = sum of units per project
  • Total Area = sum of floor area per project
  • Median Unit Price ($ psm) = median unit price per project
  • Median Transacted Price = median transacted price per project

Grouping variables: Project Name, Planning Region, Planning Area, Property Type, Type of Sale.

Two dplyr verbs do this: group_by() and summarise().

Grouped Summaries without the Pipe

realis2018_grouped <- group_by(realis2018, `Project Name`,
                               `Planning Region`, `Planning Area`,
                               `Property Type`, `Type of Sale`)

realis2018_summarised <- summarise(realis2018_grouped,
                          `Total Unit Sold`          = sum(`No. of Units`, na.rm = TRUE),
                          `Total Area`               = sum(`Area (sqm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
                          `Median Unit Price ($ psm)` = median(`Unit Price ($ psm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
                          `Median Transacted Price`  = median(`Transacted Price ($)`, na.rm = TRUE))

Note: na.rm = TRUE is required in all aggregation functions (sum(), median()) because any NA in the input would otherwise propagate to the output, silently producing missing aggregates.

This approach names every intermediate object, which gets unwieldy in longer pipelines.

Grouped Summaries with the Pipe

The pipe operator %>% passes the output of one function directly into the next, eliminating intermediate objects:

realis2018_summarised <- realis2018 %>%
  group_by(`Project Name`, `Planning Region`,
           `Planning Area`, `Property Type`,
           `Type of Sale`) %>%
  summarise(`Total Unit Sold`           = sum(`No. of Units`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Total Area`                = sum(`Area (sqm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Unit Price ($ psm)` = median(`Unit Price ($ psm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Transacted Price`   = median(`Transacted Price ($)`, na.rm = TRUE))

Preferred style: The pipe version is more readable and idiomatic in tidyverse workflows. Both produce identical results.

Designing Treemap with treemap Package

treemap() is the core function of the treemap package, offering 43+ arguments for fine-grained control.

Designing a Static Treemap

First, filter to the property type of interest:

realis2018_selected <- realis2018_summarised %>%
  filter(`Property Type` == "Condominium", `Type of Sale` == "Resale")

Using the Basic Arguments

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")

Three core arguments:

Argument Purpose Rule
index Defines the hierarchy levels Must have at least two column names; first = highest level
vSize Maps rectangle size Column must contain non-negative values only
vColor Maps rectangle colour Used together with type to determine colour encoding

Common mistake: Without specifying type, treemap() defaults to type = "index", which colours rectangles by their hierarchy group rather than by the vColor variable’s actual values. This produces a misleading result where the rectangles are coloured categorically by planning area, not by unit price intensity.

Working with vColor and type Arguments

Setting type = "value" corrects the colour encoding so rectangles reflect the numeric values of vColor:

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "value",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")
  • Rectangles now show different intensities of green reflecting median unit prices.
  • The legend is binned into equal-interval bins (e.g., 0–5000, 5000–10000, …).

Colours in treemap Package

The type argument controls how the vColor values are mapped to a colour palette. Two key types:

type Palette assumption Colour mapping
"value" Diverging palette (e.g., RdYlBu) 0 → midpoint colour; ±max(abs) → ends. Treats the palette as centred at zero.
"manual" Any palette min(values) → left end; max(values) → right end; mean(range) → midpoint. Linear mapping.

Two relevant arguments work alongside type:

  • palette: name of the ColorBrewer (or other) palette to use.
  • mapping: controls the exact value-to-colour mapping (auto-set by type).

The "value" Type Treemap

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "value",
        palette      = "RdYlBu",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")

Why no red? The “value” type maps 0 to the middle colour and negative values to the red end of RdYlBu. Since all median unit prices are positive, only the yellow-to-blue half of the palette is used, thus no red appears.

Legend range: Displays only the actual data range (e.g., 5,000–45,000) with pretty rounding, not the full palette range.

The "manual" Type Treemap

# Avoid: diverging palette with all-positive data is confusing
treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "manual",
        palette      = "RdYlBu",   # problematic for all-positive values
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")

Problem: With type = "manual", the midpoint colour (yellow in RdYlBu) is placed at mean(range(values)), and both red and blue ends appear, creating a confusing impression that some values are negative or below a threshold. Use a sequential palette instead when all values are on one side.

# Correct: sequential palette for all-positive data
treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "manual",
        palette      = "Blues",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")

Palette selection rule: Use diverging palettes (RdYlBu, RdBu) only when data has a meaningful midpoint (e.g., z-scores, change from baseline). Use sequential palettes (Blues, Greens, YlOrRd) for all-positive or all-negative data.

Treemap Layout

treemap() supports two layout algorithms:

Algorithm Reference Behaviour
"squarified" Bruls et al. (2000) Optimises aspect ratios (rectangles close to square); ignores sortID
"pivotSize" Bederson et al. (2002) Respects sortID ordering while maintaining acceptable aspect ratios. Default.

Working with the algorithm Argument

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "manual",
        palette      = "Blues",
        algorithm    = "squarified",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")

Using sortID

sortID only takes effect when algorithm = "pivotSize". It determines the order in which rectangles are placed from top-left to bottom-right:

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "manual",
        palette      = "Blues",
        algorithm    = "pivotSize",
        sortID       = "Median Transacted Price",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")

Use sortID to place the most important or highest-value items in the top-left, where the eye naturally lands first.

Designing Treemap using treemapify Package

treemapify integrates treemaps into ggplot2 using geom_treemap() as a layer. This gives access to the full ggplot2 ecosystem, including scales, themes, labels, and more.

Designing a Basic Treemap

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`),
       layout = "scol",
       start  = "bottomleft") +
  geom_treemap() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "light blue", high = "blue")

Key aesthetics:

Aesthetic Maps to
area Rectangle size (quantitative, non-negative)
fill Rectangle colour
  • layout and start control tile arrangement direction.
  • scale_fill_gradient() gives direct control over the colour ramp, without the need to choose between "value" and "manual" types.

Defining Hierarchy

treemapify uses subgroup and subgroup2 aesthetics (in aes()) to create hierarchical nesting.

Level 1. Group by Planning Region:

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area     = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill     = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup = `Planning Region`),
       start = "topleft") +
  geom_treemap()

Level 2. Group by Planning Region and Planning Area:

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area      = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill      = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup  = `Planning Region`,
           subgroup2 = `Planning Area`)) +
  geom_treemap()

Adding boundary lines between hierarchy levels:

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area      = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill      = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup  = `Planning Region`,
           subgroup2 = `Planning Area`)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_subgroup2_border(colour = "gray40", size = 2) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(colour  = "gray20")

Border geoms for hierarchy levels:

Geom Draws border around
geom_treemap_subgroup_border() Level 1 groups (subgroup)
geom_treemap_subgroup2_border() Level 2 groups (subgroup2)

Tip: Use darker or thicker borders for higher-level groups (Planning Region) and lighter/thinner borders for lower-level groups (Planning Area) to reinforce the visual hierarchy.

Designing Interactive Treemap using d3treeR

d3treeR wraps a static treemap object in a D3.js-powered interactive widget, adding drill-down zoom - clicking a rectangle zooms into that group, revealing its sub-rectangles.

Designing an Interactive Treemap

The workflow is two steps: build the static treemap, then wrap it with d3tree().

Step 1 — Build and save the static treemap:

tm <- treemap(realis2018_summarised,
              index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area"),
              vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
              vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
              type         = "value",
              title        = "Private Residential Property Sold, 2017",
              title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")
  • The treemap object tm is saved (not just plotted) by assigning it to a variable.
  • Note: realis2018_summarised (all property types) is used here, not the filtered realis2018_selected.

Step 2 — Wrap with d3tree() for interactivity:

d3tree(tm, rootname = "Singapore")
  • rootname: label shown at the top level before any drill-down.
  • Interaction: Clicking any rectangle zooms into that group; clicking the breadcrumb trail at the top zooms back out.

Key advantage: d3treeR adds drill-down navigation without requiring a different data structure — it reuses the treemap object directly.

Comparing the Three Approaches

Feature treemap treemapify d3treeR
Output Static plot Static ggplot2 Interactive HTML widget
Hierarchy levels Via index vector Via subgroup / subgroup2 Via index (inherited from treemap)
Colour type control type argument scale_fill_*() functions Inherited from treemap
Layout algorithms squarified, pivotSize Fixed (squarified default) Inherited from treemap
ggplot2 integration No Yes (full ecosystem) No
Drill-down zoom No No Yes
Border control Limited geom_treemap_*_border() Limited
Best for Quick, publication static Customised ggplot2 figures Exploratory, interactive

Key Takeaways

  1. Always specify type in treemap(). The default type = "index" colours by hierarchy group, not by vColor values.

  2. Match palette type to data. Use diverging palettes (RdYlBu) only when values span a meaningful zero. For all-positive data (like prices), use sequential palettes (Blues, YlOrRd). Using a diverging palette on one-sided data implies a false midpoint.

  3. vSize must be non-negative. Negative sizes are geometrically undefined for rectangle area, need to check the data before plotting.

  4. sortID only works with algorithm = "pivotSize" (the default). If switch to "squarified", sort order is ignored.

  5. treemapify is the ggplot2 way. It’s the best choice when you need to combine treemaps with other ggplot2 features, such as faceting, custom themes, additional annotation layers.

  6. d3treeR converts any treemap object to interactive. The two-step workflow (build tm, then d3tree(tm)) means you can prototype statically and interactivise with one extra line.

  7. Hierarchy requires at least two levels in treemap()’s index. A single-level index produces a flat (non-nested) treemap with no visible grouping structure.

Packages Reference

# Main packages
pacman::p_load(treemap, treemapify, tidyverse)

# d3treeR (GitHub only)
library(devtools)
install_github("timelyportfolio/d3treeR")
library(d3treeR)
Package Key Functions
treemap treemap()
treemapify geom_treemap(), geom_treemap_subgroup_border(), geom_treemap_subgroup2_border()
d3treeR d3tree()
dplyr (tidyverse) group_by(), summarise(), filter()
readr (tidyverse) read_csv()
ggplot2 (tidyverse) ggplot(), aes(), scale_fill_gradient()
